Block 7 · Week 3
Acousticovestibular Apparatus
LO 43D
43D — Sound Path Figures
LO 43D

Sound Energy: From External Ear to Auditory Nerve

This learning objective traces how an acoustic pressure wave in air is converted—step by step—into a neural signal the brain can interpret as sound. The process spans three anatomical regions: the external ear, the middle ear, and the inner ear (cochlea).

Sound Wave (air) → Pinna + EAM → Tympanic Membrane → Ossicles (M→I→S) → Oval Window → Cochlear Fluids → Basilar Membrane → Organ of Corti → CN VIII

Stage 1 — The External Ear: Funneling Sound (Figs. 7.92, 7.93)

The external ear consists of the pinna (auricle) and the external acoustic meatus (EAM). The pinna is shaped like a trumpet — it captures and funnels sound waves into the narrow canal. The outer one-third of the EAM is cartilaginous and the inner two-thirds is bony (petrous part of the temporal bone). At the end of this canal sits the tympanic membrane (eardrum), approximately 2.5 cm from the opening in adults.

Stage 2 — The Middle Ear: Impedance Matching (Figs. 7.94–7.97)

The tympanic membrane's vibrations are transmitted across the middle ear (tympanic cavity) by the ossicular chain:

🦴
1st Ossicle
Malleus
Handle fused to tympanic membrane; head articulates with incus
🦴
2nd Ossicle
Incus
Intermediate bridge between malleus and stapes
🦴
3rd Ossicle
Stapes
Footplate plugs into the oval window of the cochlea
🔊 Why the Ossicles Matter: Impedance Matching
Sound in air carries very little energy compared to fluid. Without the ossicles, ~99.9% of acoustic energy would be reflected at the air-fluid interface. The ossicular chain solves this with two mechanisms: (1) the area ratio — the tympanic membrane (~55 mm²) is ~17× larger than the oval window (~3.2 mm²), concentrating force; and (2) a lever action from the ossicular joints. Together they amplify pressure ~25–30 dB, allowing efficient transfer of energy into the cochlear fluids.

The middle ear also communicates with the nasopharynx via the pharyngotympanic (Eustachian) tube, which opens to equilibrate the tympanic cavity with atmospheric pressure — essential for the tympanic membrane to vibrate freely.

📐 Figures 7.94–7.97 — Middle Ear Walls
Fig. 7.94: Right middle ear after removing the tegmen tympani — superior approach, ossicles visible in position.

Fig. 7.95: Angle of cut to separate medial and lateral walls of the tympanic cavity (decalcified temporal bone dissection).

Fig. 7.96: Walls of the right tympanic cavity "opened like a book" — medial and lateral walls shown simultaneously.

Fig. 7.97: Schematic of the tympanic cavity with lateral wall opened — identifies the six named walls and their features (oval window, round window, promontory, pharyngotympanic tube opening, etc.).

Note: Figs. 7.94–7.97 were lazy-loaded in the webarchive session and are not cached as images.

Six Walls of the Tympanic Cavity (Fig. 7.97)

WallAlso CalledKey Features
RoofTegmentalTegmen tympani (thin petrous bone); separates middle ear from middle cranial fossa
FloorJugularOverlies jugular fossa; separates from internal jugular vein
MedialLabyrinthinePromontory (basal cochlear turn); oval window; round window; facial canal prominence
PosteriorMastoidAditus ad antrum → mastoid air cells; pyramidal eminence (stapedius tendon)
LateralMembranousTympanic membrane + bony walls of epitympanic recess
AnteriorCarotidPharyngotympanic tube; canal for tensor tympani; overlies carotid canal

Stage 3 — The Inner Ear: Fluid Waves & the Basilar Membrane

The stapes footplate rocks in the oval window, generating a pressure wave in the perilymph. The cochlea is a bony spiral coiled 2¾ turns around the central modiolus. Three fluid-filled compartments run the full length:

🔼
Upper Chamber
Scala Vestibuli
Begins at oval window; filled with perilymph (≈ ECF: high Na⁺, low K⁺)
🟦
Middle Chamber
Scala Media
Completely isolated; endolymph (high K⁺, low Na⁺); bounded by Reissner's membrane above, basilar membrane below
🔽
Lower Chamber
Scala Tympani
Ends at round window (pressure relief); also perilymph. Connects to scala vestibuli at apex via helicotrema
🌊 Stria Vascularis — Generating Endolymph
On the outer wall of the scala media, the stria vascularis actively pumps K⁺ into the endolymph, maintaining an extraordinary K⁺ concentration (~150 mM) and the endocochlear potential (+80 mV). This electrochemical gradient is the driving force for K⁺ to rush into hair cells when their transduction channels open.

Stage 4 — The Basilar Membrane: Tonotopic Frequency Mapping

The pressure wave in the cochlear fluids causes a traveling wave along the basilar membrane. Its mechanical properties vary continuously along the spiral:

Base of Cochlea

Basilar membrane is narrow and stiff. Peak displacement for high-frequency sounds (up to ~20,000 Hz).

Apex of Cochlea

Basilar membrane is wide and floppy. Peak displacement for low-frequency sounds (down to ~20 Hz).

⚠️ Round Window — Pressure Relief Valve
As the stapes pushes perilymph inward at the oval window, the round window (covered by the secondary tympanic membrane) bulges outward. Without this safety valve, the incompressible fluid would have nowhere to go and the basilar membrane could not deflect. Oval window input and round window output are in phase opposition.

Stage 5 — The Organ of Corti: Mechanoelectrical Transduction

Sitting on the basilar membrane is the organ of Corti. Key structural elements:

  • Inner hair cells (IHC) — 1 row; ~3,500 total; primary afferent sensory cells (~95% of auditory nerve fibers)
  • Outer hair cells (OHC) — 3 rows; ~12,000 total; cochlear amplifiers (electromotility)
  • Inner & outer pillar cells — form the rigid tunnel of Corti separating IHC from OHC; tightly woven reticular fibers
  • Reticular lamina — stiff plate into which hair cells are embedded; stereocilia project upward through it
  • Tectorial membrane — gelatinous overhanging shelf; tallest OHC stereocilia embed into it
  • Spiral ligament — anchors lateral edge of basilar membrane to cochlear wall
  • Spiral lamina — bony shelf (two plates) through which auditory nerve fibers pass to reach hair cells
  • Spiral ganglion — in the modiolus; cell bodies of cochlear nerve neurons; peripheral dendrites → hair cells; central axons → CN VIII

Stage 6 — Stereocilia & Tip Links: Opening the Transduction Channel

Each hair cell bears a staircase-shaped bundle of stereocilia connected at their tips by tiny extracellular filaments called tip links — the mechanical gate of the transduction channel.

Basilar membrane deflects upward → Stereocilia shear vs. tectorial membrane → Tip links stretch (toward tallest) → MET channels open → K⁺ flows INTO hair cell → Hair cell DEPOLARIZES
⚡ Why Potassium — Not Sodium — Depolarizes Hair Cells
Endolymph in the scala media is extraordinarily rich in K⁺ (~150 mM vs. ~5 mM in perilymph). When MET channels open at the stereociliary tips, K⁺ rushes into the cell down its electrochemical gradient, depolarizing the cell — the opposite of most neurons, where K⁺ flows out. Deflecting stereocilia away from the tallest relaxes the tip link, closes channels, and hyperpolarizes the cell.

Stage 7 — Synaptic Transmission: Hair Cell → Auditory Nerve

Hair cell depolarization triggers Ca²⁺ influx at the base of the cell, causing glutamate release from synaptic ribbons (synaptic bars) onto the afferent terminals of cochlear nerve fibers. These fibers have cell bodies in the spiral ganglion (modiolus) and their central axons form CN VIII, which enters the brainstem at the pontomedullary junction, synapsing in the cochlear nuclei.

Tonotopic Organization of the Auditory Nerve

Center of CN VIII

Fibers from the apex enter first → represent low frequencies.

Outside of CN VIII

Fibers from the base add peripherally → represent high frequencies.

💡 Clinical Pearl: Cochlear Implants
The tonotopic map of the auditory nerve is the basis of the cochlear implant. A multi-electrode array threads into the cochlea and electrically stimulates different positions — base electrodes deliver high-frequency signals, apical electrodes deliver low-frequency signals. Because the nerve itself is intact (bypassing damaged hair cells), this can restore functional hearing even in those born deaf if implanted early enough.

Common causes of hair cell loss: Presbycusis (age-related), acoustic trauma (rock concerts, industrial noise), ototoxic drugs (aminoglycosides, cisplatin).

Summary: Sound Energy Transformation — Stage by Stage

StageStructureEnergy FormKey Mechanism
1Pinna + EAMAcoustic (air)Funnels and directs sound waves to tympanic membrane
2Tympanic membraneMechanicalAir pressure vibration → membrane vibration
3Ossicles (M→I→S)MechanicalImpedance matching: amplifies pressure ~25–30 dB via area ratio + lever
4Oval window → perilymphHydraulic (fluid)Stapes footplate converts mechanical → fluid pressure wave
5Basilar membraneMechanical (displacement)Traveling wave; frequency-to-place mapping (tonotopy)
6Stereocilia / tip linksMechanical → electricalTip-link stretch opens MET channels; K⁺ influx depolarizes hair cells
7Hair cell synapseChemicalCa²⁺-triggered glutamate release onto spiral ganglion dendrites
8CN VIII (auditory nerve)Neural (APs)Tonotopic firing pattern → brainstem cochlear nuclei
